title: My Wife Liberated Me From the Plantation
summary: Interviewed by David Cline at Mount Canaan Baptist Church in Shreveport, Louisiana, in October 2013, Harry Blake recounts being born in Arkansas in 1934 to a Mississippi-born sharecropper father who could not read or write and a mother who finished third grade, and living on a plantation until he finished college at twenty-four, the year he says his wife liberated him from the plantation.

title: I Taught the Mule My Language
topic: A One-Room School and the Mule
summary: Blake describes a one-room church school taught by a college sophomore for grades one through seven, learning to read only as he neared his teens, and how, plowing mules in high school, he refused to learn mule language and instead taught the mule to stop and turn on his own spoken words paired with the pull of the reins.

title: Salt the Diet
summary: He draws lessons from his father's saying that salt in a horse's diet will make it find water on its own, applying it to making a congregation hungry for a mission, and warns, citing Jim Jones and David Koresh, that knowing how to lead means a clergyman must keep his motives right to lead people in the right direction.

title: From One-Sunday Churches to Allendale
summary: Blake names the two plantations he lived on, the Ashley place in Madison Parish near Tallulah and the Wood Spur place north of Shreveport, and traces his first rural pastorates in one-Sunday and other part-time churches before coming in 1966 to this church in the densely populated Allendale slum, where the dilapidated building he inherited later collapsed.

title: You Will Never Be My Equal
summary: Asked about relationships with white people, Blake describes a confined cordiality in which the white man's favored worker got what he needed, was kept off the World War II draft by farmers on the draft board, and was told to stay out of the cemetery and out of the white part of town, a paternalism he sums up as handing down to a man but never out to him.

title: You Owed Your Soul to the Store
summary: He lays out the commissary system in which the planter advanced food, work clothing, mules, and implements against a sharecropper's account, split a twenty-bale crop ten and ten, and at year's end declared the worker had only broken even, so a family could leave only if another farmer paid to move them, his own father somehow clearing two to five hundred dollars a year.

title: Interpret the Constitution
summary: Blake states that no black person on his plantation was registered to vote when he turned twenty, and tells how he registered at twenty-one by giving a friend's city address and studying the required literacy test, which demanded a white voter to vouch for you, identification, interpreting the United States Constitution to a registrar's satisfaction, and computing your exact age in years, months, and days, a barrier he later helped others overcome teaching voter education on Martin Luther King's staff.

part: My Daddy Taught Me to Be a Man
title: You Are a Big Dog
topic: A Father Who Worked the System
summary: Blake describes his illiterate father's gift for working the system, paying outward homage to white men he called Captain and Boss while teaching by demonstration, once telling the boss admiring his new car that he was a big dog so he could call him a dog and get by with it, never diminishing his own manhood.

title: Because My Wife Likes You
summary: He recounts a burning yearning to change what he saw was wrong, and how around 1952 in the little town of Dixie he asked store owner H.L. Whitlow for a summer job, was turned away until Whitlow's wife took a liking to him, and got hired at a store he says practiced equal employment in the fifties, with a white Louisiana Tech student and a black Grambling student both working for him.

title: What in the Hell You Think You're Worth
summary: Blake tells how, offered fifteen dollars for six longer days when the plantation paid fifteen for five, he offered to donate the week and was challenged to name his worth, demanded twenty-five dollars, and got it thrown at him, then drove Whitlow's car to school and rose to three hundred dollars a month, more than any black person he knew, under a man white friends called a nigger lover for treating people fairly.

title: The Deaf and Dumb Worker
summary: He describes refusing to answer a white postmaster-farmer who called him boy outside the store, reasoning that no law required him to respond to a man who did not sign his check, until the man told Whitlow he had found a deaf and dumb worker, an act of quiet resistance Blake calls his early beginnings of fighting the system, with his father as the model.

title: Martin Was My Commencement Speaker
summary: Blake recalls following the Baton Rouge bus boycott led by Reverend T.J. Jemison that King studied for Montgomery, reading King's Stride Toward Freedom, and having King as his commencement speaker at Bishop College in Marshall, Texas, before local leader Dr. C.O. Simkins arranged the interview that brought him onto King's staff in February 1960 to work southern Louisiana.

title: Arrested for Mental Observation
topic: First Arrest and a Bizarre Charge
summary: He recounts being arrested on his first day of work, March 1st, 1960, while sitting in a parked car downtown, fingerprinted, warned by the chief not to ruin his future fooling with the communist Martin Luther King, and charged with careless and reckless driving though no officer had seen him drive, then his most memorable arrest, when he and executive director Dr. Wyatt Tee Walker were jailed thirty-six hours for mental observation after the Klan protested a King service and NAACP youth counter-protested.

title: Four Holes in the Coat
topic: Assassination Attempt and the Hardest City
summary: He explains the authorities could only invent that he took orders from Moscow, and recounts an assassination attempt on October 12th, 1960, when a car fired a bullet that shattered his radio and left four holes in his coat, after which the sheriff reported the owner said he was bowling, then frames Shreveport, which the Civil Rights Commission called the most difficult city in the South, as a plantation environment without an immortalized Medgar Evers that kept black people disciplined to obedience.

title: I Never Taught My Daddy to Write His Name
summary: He recounts warning his parents that joining King could mean injury or death for them as well as him, his father answering he would be disappointed if Blake did anything different, learning only later that police humiliated his father by calling him a stupid nigger, and the celebration of helping his father pass the voter test set against the regret that he never taught him to write his name.

title: Five Minutes to Make My Decision
summary: After the four girls were killed in the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing and President Kennedy declared a day of mourning, Blake sought a permit for a memorial march in Shreveport on September 22nd, 1963, from Booker Washington High School to Little Union Baptist Church, was stalled until Saturday noon and refused, and was warned the public-safety commissioner would arrest everyone who marched.

title: They Left Me for Dead on the Lawn
summary: Blake describes telling people to gather and simply enter the church rather than march while police on horses with bayonets and garbage trucks filled the community, and how, going to the vestibule as the service ended, he was beaten by the commissioner and other policemen, each striking his head with a nightstick, dragged outside and left for dead on the lawn on September 22nd, 1963.

title: It Looked Like a War Zone
summary: He recounts his family fearing white doctors and sending him to Dallas, students at the high school launching a protest met with beatings, tear gas, and an arrested white teacher, Shreveport entering its greatest protest movement whose fiftieth anniversary had just been marked, and his own recovery in faculty housing arranged by the president of Bishop College.

title: A Public Proclamation of Apology
topic: D'Artois, a Jailed Student, and an Apology
summary: Blake explains that the protest came from students because parents feared the violent Commissioner D'Artois, that the city jailed one student singled out as a leader for some thirty to forty days and barred him from school until a diploma came at the fiftieth anniversary, and that forty years after the beating the city issued a public proclamation of apology that Blake keeps on his wall.

title: Property Shall Be Sold to Mount Canaan
topic: Deals With the Mayor and Land for Housing
summary: As a new pastor in 1966 Blake confronted the commissioner who beat him and the mayor, telling each he might repeat his past actions if he deemed it necessary, and the mayor, a politician who had quietly advised the churches against him, asked what he could do, leading to an ordinance that sold eight acres of city park land to Mount Canaan Baptist Church for thirty-five thousand dollars limited to housing, recreation, education, or religious use, on which the church built a 120-unit housing complex, a daycare, and later a senior citizens high-rise.

title: The Immoral Things
summary: Blake frames community work as part of the movement, which targeted immoral things like paying bus fare to stand in the back, the hamburger window, and white-only restrooms, and argues that he and King sought equal funding and capable teachers for black schools rather than the integration that bused children to white schools and closed the black ones.

title: We Got Duped
summary: He contends black people got duped by an integration they did not seek, that open hotels and restaurants mean little without a decent income, and recalls being a cashier handling money in the Whitlow country store ten to fifteen years before any black cashier appeared in the city, adding that he wants not to hold office but to determine who does.

title: A Captive Audience Every Sunday
summary: Asked about black power and allies, Blake locates lasting power in the black church, a captive Sunday audience where a leader can salt the diet, and recalls that blacks once feared King's presence in Shreveport, that several churches voted he could not preach, and that around 1961 this church voted against calling Blake for fear it would be bombed or burned.

title: How Many Prison Cells by Fourth Grade
summary: Nearing eighty and winding down, Blake describes a national crisis in schools and the statistic that prison capacity is projected from fourth-grade boys' performance, leading him to a ministry to develop strong, moral black men, a summer boys academy that he says raised one boy from a second to a sixth grade reading level.

title: We Got to Have Walter
summary: Blake tells of a twelve-year-old named Walter who could not read past fourth-grade material, prompting him to start a virtual homeschool inside the church with a recently retired teacher of more than thirty years, now serving five boys daily from eight to three, the church giving him full range to try anything that helps people.

title: Best Thing in the Center of the Hood
summary: Blake closes by describing one-on-one intervention as the heart of even a mass movement, declining thanks for himself, and calling Mount Canaan church, set in the center of the hood and giving him full range to bring about change, the best thing that ever happened to him, before the interview ends.
